audit_status.source_base: Hebrew Numbers / Bemidbar Miriam, Meribah, Edom, and Aaron-death text tradition, draft aligned later
audit_status.review_note: First-pass draft reviewed for Miriam's death, no-water crisis, speaking/striking the rock, Moses and Aaron's exclusion, Edom's refusal of passage, route change, transfer of priestly garments, Aaron's death, and communal mourning. Strong grief, anti-authoritarian, territorial-sovereignty, and anti-victim-blaming safeguards required.

verses.restored: Children of Israel, all congregation, came to wilderness of Zin in first month, and people stayed at Kadesh. Miriam died there and was buried there.
There was no water for congregation, and they assembled against Moses and Aaron.
The people contended with Moses and said, Would that we had perished when our brothers perished before YHWH!
Why have you brought assembly of YHWH into this wilderness, for us and our livestock to die there?
Why did you bring us up from Egypt to bring us to this evil place? It is no place of seed, fig, vine, or pomegranate, and there is no water to drink.
Moses and Aaron went from face of assembly to entrance of tent of meeting and fell upon their faces, and glory of YHWH appeared to them.
YHWH spoke to Moses, saying,

Take staff and assemble congregation, you and Aaron your brother, and speak to rock before their eyes, and it shall give its water. You shall bring out water for them from rock and give drink to congregation and their livestock.
Moses took staff from before YHWH as he commanded him.
Moses and Aaron assembled assembly before rock, and he said to them, Hear, please, rebels! From this rock shall we bring out water for you?
Moses lifted his hand and struck rock with his staff twice; abundant water came out, and congregation and their livestock drank.
YHWH said to Moses and Aaron, Because you did not trust me to sanctify me before eyes of children of Israel, therefore you shall not bring this assembly into land that I have given them.
These are waters of Meribah/Contention, where children of Israel contended with YHWH, and he was sanctified among them.
Moses sent messengers from Kadesh to king of Edom: Thus says your brother Israel: You know all hardship that found us.
Our fathers went down to Egypt, and we dwelt in Egypt many days; Egyptians did evil to us and our fathers.
We cried to YHWH, and he heard our voice and sent a messenger and brought us out from Egypt. And look, we are at Kadesh, a city at edge of your border.

Please let us pass through your land. We will not pass through field or vineyard, and we will not drink water of a well; by king's road we will go, not turning right or left until we pass through your border.
Edom said to him, You shall not pass through me, lest I come out with sword to meet you.
Children of Israel said to him, By highway we will go up, and if we drink your water, I and my livestock, I will give its price; only let me pass through on my feet, nothing more.
He said, You shall not pass. Edom came out to meet him with heavy people and strong hand.
Edom refused to allow Israel to pass through his territory, and Israel turned away from him.
They set out from Kadesh, and children of Israel, all congregation, came to Mount Hor.
YHWH spoke to Moses and Aaron at Mount Hor, by border of land of Edom, saying,
Aaron shall be gathered to his people, for he shall not enter land I have given to children of Israel, because you rebelled against my mouth/command at waters of Meribah.
Take Aaron and Eleazar his son, and bring them up Mount Hor.
Strip Aaron of his garments and clothe Eleazar his son with them; Aaron shall be gathered and die there.
Moses did as YHWH commanded, and they went up Mount Hor before eyes of all congregation.
Moses stripped Aaron of his garments and clothed Eleazar his son with them; Aaron died there on top of mountain. Moses and Eleazar came down from mountain.
All congregation saw that Aaron had died, and all house of Israel wept for Aaron thirty days.

translation_notes: Miriam's death is stated briefly, yet it opens a chapter dominated by water crisis, leadership strain, and the death of Aaron; grief must remain visible even where the narrative is terse.
The people's demand for water is materially legitimate. Their rhetoric may be accusatory, but thirst is not a moral failure.
YHWH commands Moses to speak to the rock; Moses instead addresses the people as rebels and strikes twice. The text holds leaders accountable even when water is still provided.
Moses and Aaron's exclusion must not be used to claim one mistake erases a person's whole life or service.
Edom has an existing border and refuses passage. Israel turns away rather than forcing entry, an important limit on movement through another people's territory.
Aaron's garments pass to Eleazar before Aaron dies, separating continuity of office from immortality of officeholder.
Thirty days of communal mourning recognize Aaron's death as a shared loss.
safety_note: Do not use Numbers 20 to shame people for needing water, excuse leaders who lash out under pressure, erase Miriam's importance, override another people's territorial refusal, or teach that grief must be brief and tidy. The chapter holds need, failure, boundary, succession, and mourning together.
